data.when-triage-picks-it#When triage picks i:
Triage selects this archetype when the ticket implies an architectural decision rather than (or in addition to) a code change. Trigger signals include:
Action verbs: adopt, migrate, replace, switch to, move away from
Introduction of a new technology, library, or infrastructure component not currently in the codebase
Changes to data models, API contracts, or cross-service boundaries
Infrastructure topology changes (caching layer, queue, storage backend, auth provider)
Triage may stack the ADR archetype before generate-spec and self-healing when the ticket implies both a decision and an implementation.
data.how-it-works#How it works:
The adr-author agent (Sonnet) runs a 12-step flow:
Reads the ticket and classifies the decision type.
Scans the codebase and .rules/AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md for existing constraints that bear on the decision.
Enumerates alternatives considered (at least three).
Analyzes the diff surface for PR conflicts with open branches.
Evaluates each alternative against documented project conventions.
Selects a recommendation and records the rationale.
Writes the ADR to the standard format under docs/decisions/ (or the path your project uses).
If the selected option conflicts with a documented rule, the adr_rule_violation_gate fires — this gate cannot be set to proceed. If the diff analysis reveals high conflict potential, the adr_conflict_gate fires per your autonomy preset.
data.real-world-ticket-example#Real-world tic: do "Use Redis for session caching instead of the current in-memory store — we need sessions to survive pod restarts"

data.when-triage-picks-it#When triage picks i: Triage selects this archetype when a development ticket implies code work but no formal contract exists yet. The signal is a ticket that describes desired behavior — a feature, a component, an endpoint, a migration — without specifying the exact files, interfaces, or acceptance criteria needed to implement it safely. Triage refuses to send workers into code without a spec.
data.how-it-works#How it works:
A single-phase pipeline runs three agents in sequence:
spec-refiner (Haiku) scans the ticket and the existing codebase context, then fills out the 20-section spec template section by section, flagging gaps it cannot resolve from available information.
spec-validator (Sonnet) reviews the draft and returns one of three verdicts: PASS, WARNINGS, or BLOCK. A BLOCK triggers the spec_clarification_gate.
On PASS or WARNINGS, the spec is written to .loop/ and handed off as the contract for the subsequent self-healing phase.
The output is a machine-readable spec that worker-dev agents consume directly — no human reformatting required.
data.real-world-ticket-example#Real-world tic: do "I want to add a dark mode toggle to the dashboard — it should persist the user's preference across sessions"

data.when-triage-picks-it#When triage picks i: Triage selects this archetype when the ticket contains N independent sub-tasks that carry no data dependency on each other — meaning all of them can start at the same time. Common signals: explicit enumeration ("research X options", "compare Y libraries"), audit-style requests, or any ticket where the result is a synthesis of parallel findings.
data.how-it-works#How it works: The orchestrator decomposes the ticket into a set of worker assignments. Each worker runs in isolation — separate sub-agent context, separate git worktree when the task involves code. Workers execute in parallel waves. When all workers in a wave complete, their outputs are merged and handed to the spec-writer (Opus) for final synthesis and artifact assembly.The number of waves depends on whether any later tasks depend on the merged output of earlier ones. Pure fan-out tickets run in a single wave.
data.real-world-ticket-example#Real-world tic: do "Research 5 JavaScript bundlers (Webpack, Vite, esbuild, Rollup, Parcel) and produce a comparison table covering build speed, bundle size, config complexity, and ecosystem maturity"

data.when-triage-picks-it#When triage picks i: Triage selects this archetype for any code-writing phase — when the ticket requires creating or modifying source files and the project has discoverable verification commands (lint, typecheck, tests, build). It is almost always preceded by a generate-spec phase; the spec becomes the contract the workers implement.
data.how-it-works#How it works: Each file assignment is dispatched to a worker-dev sub-agent running in an isolated git worktree. Workers operate in parallel, one file per invocation. When workers complete, the verifier runs the project's actual verification suite — no mocked checks.If a verification command fails, the loop identifies which file caused the failure and re-dispatches a corrective worker with the failure output attached. The worker edits only the minimum necessary to fix the named error. This retry cycle runs up to three times per file. If the file still fails after the maximum retries, the self_healing_exhaust_gate fires according to your autonomy preset.Your HEAD is invariant throughout — the plugin never touches your working tree.
data.real-world-ticket-example#Real-world tic: do "Implement the login form component with unit tests and integration tests — email + password fields, validation, error states, and a loading spinner on submit"

data: All three presets shown as complete, ready-to-use .loop/config.yaml files. Pick one and drop it in your repo root.
data.minimal-preset#Minimal Preset:
Total autonomy. The plugin only stops for hard safety-floor events (rule violations, hotfix detection). Ideal for repetitive tickets after a calibration period.
# .loop/config.yaml
autonomy:
  preset: minimal   # Plugin proceeds on all gates except the safety floor.
                    # Budget overruns: ignored up to the global cap.
                    # ADR conflicts: logged in the ADR, run continues.
                    # Self-healing exhausted: added to STATE, no interruption.
                    # Spec gaps: marked TBD, not asked.
                    # PR creation: draft PR opened automatically.
                    # Rule violations + hotfix: ALWAYS ask (non-configurable floor).
data.ownership-preset#Ownership Preset:
Human owns architectural and risk decisions; the plugin owns routine execution. Expect 3–5 interactions per development ticket, at the moments that matter.
# .loop/config.yaml
autonomy:
  preset: ownership # Human is consulted for:
                    #   - ADR conflicts with open PRs (adr_conflict_gate → ask)
                    #   - Self-healing exhausted after retries (self_healing_exhaust_gate → ask)
                    #   - PR ready to be created (pr_creation_gate → ask)
                    #   - Rule violations + hotfix (safety floor — always ask)
                    # Plugin proceeds autonomously for:
                    #   - Small budget deltas
                    #   - Verifier auto-fix for lint/type/test failures (2 retries)
                    #   - Spec gaps (marked TBD)
                    #   - Ticket status updates
data.custom-preset#Custom Preset:
You define every gate explicitly. Every gate listed below is required — the plugin rejects partial custom configs at boot.
# .loop/config.yaml
autonomy:
  preset: custom    # ALL gates must be defined. Missing any → boot error.
gates:
  budget_gate:
    enabled: true
    threshold_usd: 2.00       # Ask if the plan estimates spending more than $2.00.
    threshold_tokens: 150000  # Ask if the plan estimates more than 150k tokens.
    on_exceed: ask            # ask | proceed | abort
  verifier_blocking_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_blocking: auto_fix     # ask | auto_fix | proceed_with_warnings | abort
    auto_fix_max_retries: 3   # 0–5. Values > 5 are rejected (infinite-loop prevention).
  adr_conflict_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_high_conflict: ask     # ask | proceed_with_record | abort
  adr_rule_violation_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_violation: ask         # ask | abort. "proceed" is REJECTED — rules are non-negotiable.
  spec_clarification_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_gaps: ask              # ask | use_defaults_and_flag | abort
    max_questions: 3          # 0–20. Values > 20 are rejected (friction cap).
  self_healing_exhaust_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_exhausted: ask         # ask | escalate_silent | abort
  ticket_status_update_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_complete: proceed      # ask | proceed | proceed_with_record | skip
  pr_creation_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_ready: ask             # ask | proceed (always draft) | skip
  notification_gate:
    enabled: true             # true | false
    on_complete: proceed      # proceed | skip

data: Triage picks the right archetype automatically. These examples show what kinds of tickets trigger each one — copy any of them verbatim.
data.orchestrator-workers#Orchestrator-Worker:
Use when the ticket has N independent sub-tasks that can run in parallel and don't depend on each other's output.
do "Research and compare Vite, esbuild, Turbopack, Rollup, and Parcel: bundle size, HMR speed, ecosystem support. Produce a comparison table."
data.plan-execute#Plan-Execute:
Use when the ticket is a sequential pipeline where each step consumes the output of the previous one.
do "Extract all user records from the legacy PostgreSQL schema, transform them to the new UserProfile shape, and load them into the v2 database."
data.self-healing#Self-Healing:
Use for any code-writing ticket — workers run in isolated git worktrees, the verifier executes lint/typecheck/tests, and failed files are retried automatically (up to 3 times).
do "Implement the EmailVerificationService: send token on signup, verify endpoint, expiry logic. Include unit tests."
data.generate-spec#Generate-Spec:
Use when the ticket implies a non-trivial feature and you want a validated 20-section spec before any code is written. Triage picks this when it detects implementation scope without sufficient acceptance criteria.
do "Add multi-factor authentication to the login flow: TOTP and SMS fallback. Generate spec before coding."
data.adr#ADR:
Use when the ticket contains architectural verbs — adopt, migrate, replace, introduce — or proposes a new technology, data model, API design, or infrastructure change.
do "Adopt Redis for session caching and replace the current in-memory store. Document the decision with trade-offs."

data: Ticket in. Deliverable out.somnio-loop is a Claude Code plugin that runs a full agentic loop end-to-end without further input. Hand it a ticket — a free-text prompt, a Jira/Linear/GitHub Issues ID, or a paragraph pasted directly — and it handles triage, parallel sub-agent fan-out, self-healing verification, spec generation, and ADR recording automatically. It is technology-agnostic and works on any stack with a discoverable manifest.For the current release, see the CHANGELOG.Get started →
Get somnio-loop running in under five minutes. See CHANGELOG.md for the current release version.
data.prerequisites#Prerequisites:
Before installing, confirm you have the following:
Claude Code CLI — installed and authenticated
Node.js 20 or later
pnpm or npm (for any project-level tooling)
data.installation#Installation:
Run these three commands once. No project-specific setup is required to get started.
# 1) Register the marketplace source
claude plugin marketplace add giosomniodev/somnio-loop
# 2) Install the plugin
claude plugin install somnio-loop@somnio-loop
# 3) Confirm it is active
claude plugin list | grep somnio-loop
data.first-run#First Run:
Open Claude Code inside any project directory and invoke the do command with a plain-text prompt:
somnio-loop:do "Investigate the last 3 TypeScript releases and write a 300-word summary."
The plugin surfaces its resolved stance immediately:
🎚 Readiness level: L1 (report-only) — first run on this repo.
🎛 Autonomy: balanced (default from .loop/config.yaml)
From there it runs triage, fans out workers, executes the verifier, and delivers a consolidated artifact with a per-agent consumption table. For a research prompt of this size, expect roughly 3–5 minutes and $0.30–$0.50 USD.
data.minimal-config#Minimal Config:
On first run, somnio-loop creates .loop/config.yaml automatically. You only need to edit it to connect a ticket tracker or change the autonomy preset. A complete working config looks like this:
# .loop/config.yaml
autonomy:
  preset: minimal   # or ownership or custom
mcp_integrations:
  ticket_source:
    type: jira
    project_key: "PROJ"
  pr_target:
    type: github
    owner: "your-org"
    repo: "your-repo"
    base_branch: "develop"
The preset drives all gate defaults — you never need to spell out individual gate values unless you choose preset: custom. Observability (surface lines, per-agent consumption table, run report, STATE updates) is always on regardless of preset.For the full gate reference and per-preset behavior, see Autonomy & Gates.

data: The .loop/config.yaml file is the single control surface for how much autonomy the plugin exercises. It is read at every Phase -1 before any agent is dispatched.
data.presets#Presets:
Set autonomy.preset to one of three values. That is usually the only line you need.
Preset	Who decides	Typical interactions per ticket	minimal	Plugin decides everything except safety floors	0–1	ownership	Human owns architecture, risk, and delivery moments	3–5	custom	Every gate defined explicitly by the user	varies	
# .loop/config.yaml
autonomy:
  preset: ownership
Presets are self-contained. Setting preset: minimal implies all gate defaults — you do not need to list each gate.
data.preset-expansion#Preset expansion: Gate	minimal	ownership	budget_gate.on_exceed	proceed	ask (only if >2x threshold)	verifier_blocking_gate.on_blocking	auto_fix (2 retries)	auto_fix (2 retries)	adr_conflict_gate.on_high_conflict	proceed_with_record	ask	adr_rule_violation_gate.on_violation	ask (safety floor)	ask (safety floor)	spec_clarification_gate.on_gaps	use_defaults_and_flag	use_defaults_and_flag	self_healing_exhaust_gate.on_exhausted	escalate_silent	ask	pr_creation_gate.on_ready	proceed (draft only)	ask	notification_gate.enabled	false	false
data.custom-preset#Custom preset:
If you set preset: custom, every gate must be defined explicitly. Partial configs are rejected at Phase -1.
autonomy:
  preset: custom
gates:
  budget_gate:
    enabled: true
    threshold_usd: 2.00
    on_exceed: ask           # ask | proceed | abort
  verifier_blocking_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_blocking: auto_fix
    auto_fix_max_retries: 2  # max 5; higher values rejected
  adr_conflict_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_high_conflict: ask    # ask | proceed_with_record | abort
  adr_rule_violation_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_violation: ask        # ask | abort — "proceed" is REJECTED
  spec_clarification_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_gaps: use_defaults_and_flag
    max_questions: 0         # max 20
  self_healing_exhaust_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_exhausted: escalate_silent
  ticket_status_update_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_complete: proceed
  pr_creation_gate:
    enabled: true
    on_ready: ask
  notification_gate:
    enabled: false
    on_complete: skip
data.gate-actions#Gate actions: Action	Effect	ask	Run pauses; user presented 2–3 options	proceed	Continue on default safe path; decision recorded	proceed_with_record	Continue; item added to STATE Watch List	proceed_with_warnings	Continue; run marked status: partial	auto_fix	Corrective worker dispatched; capped at auto_fix_max_retries	escalate_silent	Item added to STATE High Priority; no interrupt	abort	Run stops; status set to aborted
data.per-run-override#Per-run override:
Append --autonomy=<preset> to any ticket invocation. The override applies to that run only; the config file is not modified.
do "Migrate auth to Riverpod --autonomy=minimal"
data.safety-floors#Safety floors:
These four rules apply regardless of preset and cannot be configured away:
PRs are never auto-merged.
Nothing is ever pushed to main, master, or protected branches.
Denylist paths from budget.md are never written to.
Documented rule violations (adr_rule_violation_gate) can only trigger ask or abort — never proceed.

data: The plugin classifies every code-writing ticket into one of eight types and applies the corresponding branch naming, base branch, and PR target automatically.
data.ticket-types-at-a-glance#Ticket types at:
Type	Branch pattern	Base	PR target	Commit prefix	feature	feature/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	feat:	bugfix	bugfix/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	fix:	hotfix	hotfix/{ticket_id}-{slug}	main	main + back-merge to develop	fix:	release	release/{version}	develop	main + back-merge to develop	chore(release):	chore	chore/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	chore:	docs	docs/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	docs:	refactor	refactor/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	refactor:	spike	spike/{ticket_id}-{slug}	develop	develop	chore(spike):	
Slugs are kebab-case English, max 50 characters.
data.classification-signals#Classification si:
Triage evaluates signals top-to-bottom; first match wins.
Type	Key signals	hotfix	"hotfix", "P0", "P1", "production down", "URGENT", branch fix needed on main	release	"release v", "cut release", "prepare release", title matches release\s+v?\d	bugfix	"fix", "bug", "regression", "resolve" — no production urgency	docs	Only doc verbs + only .md/docs/ paths mentioned	chore	Dependency bumps, CI config, tooling changes, no application logic	refactor	"refactor", "rename", "extract", "no new behavior"	spike	"spike", "POC", "investigate", "prototype", "throwaway"	feature	Anything else with new-functionality verbs (add, build, implement)
data.configuration#Configuration:
# .loop/config.yaml
git_flow:
  enabled: true
  base_branch: develop
  main_branch: main
  branch_patterns:
    feature:  "feature/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    bugfix:   "bugfix/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    hotfix:   "hotfix/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    release:  "release/{version}"
    chore:    "chore/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    docs:     "docs/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    refactor: "refactor/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    spike:    "spike/{ticket_id}-{slug}"
  commit_message:
    style: conventional   # conventional | simple | none
  follow_up:
    hotfix_back_merge_to_develop: true
    release_back_merge_to_develop: true
data.alternative-strategies#Alternative strat:
GitHub Flow (main only):
git_flow:
  base_branch: main
  main_branch: main
  pr_targets:
    feature: main
    bugfix: main
    hotfix: main
    # ... all types → main
  follow_up:
    hotfix_back_merge_to_develop: false
    release_back_merge_to_develop: false
Trunk-based development (no branch type prefixes):
git_flow:
  base_branch: main
  branch_patterns:
    feature:  "{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    bugfix:   "{ticket_id}-{slug}"
    # ... all types use simple pattern
data.how-the-plugin-creates-branches#How the:
The plugin never touches your current HEAD. It uses a dedicated worktree for all writes and verifies the invariant before and after the run.
1. Snapshot user HEAD and current branch
2. Resolve base branch for this ticket type
3. Create new branch pointing to base (no checkout)
4. Open a dedicated merge worktree on the new branch
5. Apply each worker's diff into the merge worktree
6. Commit with conventional message
7. Remove all worktrees
8. Assert user HEAD unchanged — abort with INVARIANT VIOLATED if not
data.conventional-commit-format#Conventional:
feat(PROJ-127): add Redis cache for session state
Introduces Redis-backed session storage with TTL-based eviction.
Replaces previous in-memory session store.
Closes PROJ-127
data.hotfix-safety#Hotfix safety: Even with autonomy: minimal, the plugin always surfaces a confirmation before creating a hotfix/* branch from main. Hotfixes touch production paths and this friction is non-negotiable.
data.disabling-git-flow#Disabling Git Flow: Set git_flow.enabled: false to revert to the simpler loop/<ticket_id> branch model with no type classification.

data: The triage agent assigns a readiness level to every ticket. The level gates what the plugin will and will not do during that run.
data.the-four-levels#The four levels: Level	Name	What it does	What it does not do	L0	Draft	Produces a plan.yml and a one-page intent report. No agent dispatch beyond triage.	Any write to user-facing artifacts.	L1	Report	Runs research, discovery, and audit phases. Reads code. Produces analysis reports.	Code writes, spec writes, ADR writes, git operations.	L2	Assisted	Produces specs, ADRs, and code changes in worktrees. Every write goes through an approval gate before committing.	Auto-merging PRs or pushing to protected branches.	L3	Unattended	Full autonomy. No approval gate. Self-healing converges without human input.	Auto-merging PRs (never, even L3). Writing to denylist paths.
data.per-level-capability-table#Per-level cap: Capability	L0	L1	L2	L3	Triage emits plan	yes	yes	yes	yes	Sub-agent dispatch	no	yes (research only)	yes	yes	Spec write to .planning/	no	no	yes (after approval)	yes	ADR write to docs/adr/	no	no	yes (after approval)	yes	Worker writes to code paths	no	no	yes (in worktree)	yes (in worktree)	Self-healing retries	no	no	yes (max 3)	yes (max 3)	PR description write	no	no	yes	yes	Append to .loop/state.md	yes	yes	yes	yes	Append to .loop/run-log.md	yes	yes	yes	yes	Touch denylist paths	never	never	never	never	Merge to main / protected branches	never	never	never	never
data.denylist-paths-all-levels#Denylist paths:
These paths are never written to, regardless of level or autonomy setting:
.env, .env.*
secrets/, secret/, private/
infra/production/, terraform/production/, k8s/production/
.git/objects/, .git/hooks/
Any path starting with .ssh/, .aws/, .gcloud/, .kube/
data.how-triage-assigns-a-level#How triage as:
Rules are evaluated in order; first match wins.
Rule	Outcome	User explicitly requested a level in the ticket	Honor it	Ticket touches denylist paths	Cap at L1 regardless of request	Run log shows 3+ successful runs of this archetype	Default L2 (or L3 if prior runs were marked success_no_review_needed)	Run log shows 1+ successful runs of this archetype	Default L2 with approval gate	No history or fewer than 1 successful run	Default L1	Ticket is purely conversational ("explain X")	L0 — no agents needed	
After picking a level, triage tells the user which level was chosen and why, then offers to downshift via AskUserQuestion. Upshifting requires explicit confirmation.
data.recommended-rollout#Recommended rollout:
Period	Approach	Week 1	Run all tickets at L1. Review artifacts manually. Build trust.	Week 2	For ticket types reviewed successfully twice, move to L2.	Week 3+	For trivial recurring tickets (rename, typo, single-file), move to L3.	
The plugin surfaces the data from .loop/run-log.md and makes recommendations. You decide when to advance.

data: The plugin maintains all persistent state inside a single directory at your repo root: .loop/. Every file is plain Markdown or YAML — readable and diffable without the plugin.
data.directory-layout#Directory layout:
<repo_root>/.loop/
├── state.md            # Durable run state — read at start, updated at end
├── run-log.md          # Append-only history, one entry per run
├── budget.md           # Daily/weekly caps and kill switch
├── config.yaml         # Autonomy presets and gate behavior
├── traces/             # Machine-readable JSON traces, one per run
│   └── <ts>-<ticket_hash>.json
└── plans/              # Archived plan.yml per run
    └── <ts>-<ticket_hash>.yml
If .loop/ does not exist when a run starts, the plugin creates it from templates.
data.statemd#state.md:
Tracks high-priority items waiting on human input, a watch list, and a conventions snapshot used as a fast cache between runs.
# Loop state — <project-name>
Last run: <ISO-8601>
Last status: completed | partial | aborted
Last readiness level: L1 | L2 | L3
## High Priority (waiting on human)
- [ ] **PROJ-127** — Redis cache spike blocked on infra approval. Last touched: 2024-01-15T10:00Z.
## Watch List
- **PROJ-128** — Auth refactor. Reason: ADR conflict with open PR #45.
## Conventions snapshot
- project_type: backend
- sub_type: nestjs
- task_runner: Makefile
- verification:
    lint: make lint
    typecheck: make typecheck
    test: make test
data.run-logmd#run-log.md:
Append-only. The plugin never edits or deletes existing entries.
## 2024-01-15T10:32:00Z — PROJ-127
- **Status:** completed
- **Readiness level:** L2
- **Duration:** 4m 12s
- **Tokens:** 84,300 total
- **Cost estimate:** $0.42
- **Artifacts produced:**
  - src/cache/redis-session.ts
  - docs/adr/ADR-001-redis-session.md
- **Self-healing retries:** 1
- **Trace:** .loop/traces/20240115T103200-proj127.json
data.budgetmd#budget.md:
daily:
  max_tokens: 500000
  max_cost_usd: 5.00
  max_runs: 20
weekly:
  max_tokens: 2000000
  max_cost_usd: 25.00
per_run:
  max_tokens: 250000
  max_cost_usd: 3.00
  max_duration_seconds: 1800
kill_switch:
  enabled: false
  reason: ""
  paused_until: ""    # ISO-8601 timestamp to auto-resume
denylist_paths:
  - .env
  - .env.*
  - secrets/
  - infra/production/
Setting any cap to 0 disables the plugin until manually reset. Setting kill_switch.enabled: true shows the reason in chat and refuses to run.
data.how-the-plugin-uses-loop#How the plugin: Moment	Action	Run start	Read state.md, tail of run-log.md (last 10), and budget.md. Abort if budget exhausted or kill switch active.	After plan emitted	Validate all worker write paths against denylist_paths. Reject plan if any path matches.	Run end	Append entry to run-log.md. Update state.md. Save trace.json and plan.yml archives.
data.version-control#Version control: Add .loop/ to .gitignore for solo use. Commit it if you want to share run history and state across a team — the schema is review-friendly plain text.

data: This reference is used by worker-dev, verifier, loop-self-healing, and spec-refiner to find the commands that verify code in a given project.
data.discovery-order#Discovery order:
Commands are resolved in priority order. Lower levels are only used when higher levels yield nothing.
Priority	Source	How	1 — Highest	Project task runner	Read Makefile, justfile, Taskfile.yml, tasks.py, Rakefile, mix.exs aliases, lefthook.yml	2	Manifest scripts section	package.json → scripts, pyproject.toml → tool scripts, composer.json → scripts	3 — Fallback	Idiomatic per-language defaults	Table below — only when steps 1 and 2 yield nothing
data.typescript--javascript#TypeScript / Java: Action	npm	pnpm	yarn	bun	Typecheck	npx tsc --noEmit	pnpm exec tsc --noEmit	yarn tsc --noEmit	bun x tsc --noEmit	Test	npx jest / npx vitest run	pnpm test	yarn test	bun test	Lint	npx eslint .	pnpm lint	yarn lint	bun lint	Build	npx tsc / npx vite build	pnpm build	yarn build	bun run build
data.python#Python: Action	Command	Typecheck	mypy <package> / pyright	Test	pytest / nox / tox	Lint	ruff check / flake8 / pylint	Format check	ruff format --check . / black --check .
data.go#Go: Action	Command	Build / Typecheck	go build ./...	Test	go test ./...	Lint	golangci-lint run / go vet ./...	Format check	gofmt -l .
data.rust#Rust: Action	Command	Typecheck	cargo check --all-targets	Test	cargo test --all	Lint	cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings	Format check	cargo fmt --check
data.ruby#Ruby: Action	Command	Test	bundle exec rspec	Lint	bundle exec rubocop
data.java#Java: Action	Maven	Gradle	Test	mvn test	./gradlew test	Lint	mvn checkstyle:check	./gradlew checkstyleMain	Build	mvn package	./gradlew build
data.kotlin#Kotlin: Action	Command	Test	./gradlew test	Lint	./gradlew ktlintCheck / ./gradlew detekt
data.net-c--f#.NET (C# / F#): Action	Command	Test	dotnet test	Lint	dotnet format --verify-no-changes	Build	dotnet build
data.elixir#Elixir: Action	Command	Test	mix test	Lint	mix credo --strict	Format check	mix format --check-formatted	Build	mix compile --warnings-as-errors
data.php#PHP: Action	Command	Test	vendor/bin/phpunit / vendor/bin/pest	Lint	vendor/bin/phpstan analyse	Format check	vendor/bin/pint --test
data.flutter--dart#Flutter / Dart: Action	Command	Test	flutter test / dart test	Lint	flutter analyze / dart analyze	Format check	dart format --set-exit-if-changed .
data.swift-ios#Swift (iOS): Action	Command	Test	xcodebuild test -scheme <scheme> -destination '...' / swift test	Lint	swiftlint lint	Format check	swiftformat --lint .
data.monorepo-orchestrators#Monorepo orchestr: Tool	Command	Nx	nx run-many --target=test --all	Turborepo	turbo run test	pnpm workspace	pnpm -r test	Bazel	bazel test //...
data.hard-rule#Hard rule: When authoring spec §8, always prefer commands from the project's own task runner or manifest scripts. The fallback table is a last resort for new projects without configured scripts. If even the fallback does not apply, mark §8 with TBD — verification commands missing; clarify with user before implementation.

data.overview#Overview: The do skill is the main entry point for the somnio-loop plugin. It accepts a ticket or task description and orchestrates the full agentic loop end-to-end — from triage and planning through implementation, verification, and final artifact delivery.
data.when-to-use#When to use:
User pastes a Jira/Linear/GitHub ticket or a free-form task description
User says /loop:do, "run this end to end", or "execute the full flow"
Any request that implies a multi-phase deliverable handed off to autonomous execution
Do NOT use for single-step lookups or quick edits — those run without loop overhead.
data.inputs#Inputs: Input	Description	Ticket text	Full ticket body, bullet list, or bare ticket ID (e.g. TKT-123)	.loop/config.yaml	Autonomy preset and gate configuration	.loop/state.md	Prior run context and watch list	--autonomy=<preset>	Optional per-run override at the end of the ticket
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Location	Final deliverable(s)	User's outputs folder	run-report.md	Alongside every artifact	plan.yml	.loop/plans/<ts>-<ticket_hash>.yml	trace.json	.loop/traces/<ts>-<ticket_hash>.json	Updated run-log	.loop/run-log.md	
The final chat message always includes an executive summary and a per-agent consumption table (model, invocations, time, tokens, cost).
data.example#Example:
/loop:do TKT-421 — Add rate limiting to the public API
Acceptance criteria:
- 429 returned after 100 req/min per API key
- X-RateLimit-* headers on every response
- Existing integration tests still pass

data.when-triage-picks-it#When triage picks i: Triage selects this archetype when the ticket describes a sequential pipeline where each step consumes the output of the previous one. The hallmark is explicit data flow: extract → transform → store, or scan → classify → report. Steps cannot be parallelized because step N+1 requires the result of step N.
data.how-it-works#How it works: Triage produces a plan.yml with ordered phases and declared inputs/outputs per phase. The runtime executes phases one by one, passing the output artifact of each phase as the named input to the next. If a phase fails, the pipeline stops at that point — earlier phase outputs are preserved in .loop/ for inspection and resumption.The final artifact is assembled by the spec-writer once all phases complete, combining the chain of outputs into a single coherent deliverable.
data.real-world-ticket-example#Real-world tic: do "Extract all TODO and FIXME comments from the codebase, group them by file and severity, then write a prioritized backlog in Markdown with effort estimates"

data: The plugin discovers MCP tools at runtime via ToolSearch. These config blocks tell it which vendor to prefer and how to behave. Replace every placeholder with your actual values — never commit real credentials.
data.jira-integration#Jira Integration:
# .loop/config.yaml — mcp_integrations block (Jira + GitHub)
mcp_integrations:
  ticket_source:
    type: jira
    cloud_id: "YOUR_JIRA_CLOUD_ID"          # Leave blank to auto-discover via getAccessibleAtlassianResources.
    project_key: "MYP"                       # Used to filter JQL searches and parse ticket IDs.
    required: false                          # If true, the plugin aborts when Jira MCP is unavailable.
    update_status_on_complete: true          # Set ticket to status_in_review after a successful run.
    status_in_progress: "In Progress"        # Jira status name for "work started".
    status_in_review: "In Review"            # Jira status name for "PR created". Never "Done".
    custom_field_acceptance_criteria: "YOUR_JIRA_AC_FIELD_ID"  # e.g. "customfield_10034"
    comment_template: "default"             # Or a path to a .md template inside your repo.
  pr_target:
    type: github
    owner: "your-org"
    repo: "your-repo"
    base_branch: "develop"
    draft_default: true                      # PRs are always opened as drafts. Non-configurable floor.
    pr_template_path: ".github/pull_request_template.md"
Credentials (the API key, Jira base URL, OAuth token) are configured in Claude Code's MCP server settings — not in this file. Use YOUR_JIRA_API_KEY, YOUR_JIRA_BASE_URL as placeholders in your MCP server config.
data.linear-integration#Linear Integration:
# .loop/config.yaml — mcp_integrations block (Linear + GitHub)
mcp_integrations:
  ticket_source:
    type: linear
    project_key: "ENG"                       # Linear team identifier used for issue filtering.
    required: false
    update_status_on_complete: true
    status_in_progress: "In Progress"
    status_in_review: "In Review"
    comment_template: "default"
  pr_target:
    type: github
    owner: "your-org"
    repo: "your-repo"
    base_branch: "main"
    draft_default: true
    pr_template_path: ".github/pull_request_template.md"
  notification:
    type: slack
    channel: "#eng-loops"                    # Slack channel to post run summaries.
    notify_on: [completed, partial, aborted] # Event types that trigger a notification.
    summary_template: "default"
Set YOUR_LINEAR_API_KEY in your Linear MCP server config inside Claude Code — not here.
data.github-integration#GitHub Integration:
# .loop/config.yaml — mcp_integrations block (GitHub Issues as ticket source + GitHub PRs)
mcp_integrations:
  ticket_source:
    type: github_issues
    owner: "your-org"                        # GitHub org or user that owns the repo.
    repo: "your-repo"                        # Repository where issues live.
    required: false
    update_status_on_complete: true
    status_in_review: "In Review"            # Applied as a label — GitHub has no native status field.
    comment_template: "default"
  pr_target:
    type: github
    owner: "your-org"
    repo: "your-repo"
    base_branch: "develop"
    draft_default: true
    pr_template_path: ".github/pull_request_template.md"
  notification:
    type: slack
    channel: "#deploys"
    notify_on: [completed]
    summary_template: "default"
Set YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN in your GitHub MCP server config inside Claude Code. The token needs repo scope for PR creation and issue reads.

data: Every run writes two artifacts to .loop/: a human-readable run-report.md entry appended to run-log.md, and a machine-readable trace.json in .loop/traces/. All content below is illustrative.
data.run-logmd-entry#run-log.md entry:
## 2026-07-01T14:32:11Z — MYP-204
- **Status:** completed
- **Readiness level:** L2
- **Archetype phases:** generate-spec → self-healing
- **Duration:** 4m 18s
- **Tokens:** 187,430 total — Haiku: 42,100, Sonnet: 131,200, Opus: 14,130
- **Cost estimate:** $0.44
- **Artifacts produced:**
  - .planning/phases/MYP-204-spec.md
  - src/services/EmailVerificationService.ts
  - src/services/__tests__/EmailVerificationService.test.ts
- **Verifier blocking findings:** 0
- **Self-healing retries:** 1
- **User overrides:** none
- **Trace:** .loop/traces/20260701T143211-myp204.json
- **Plan archive:** .loop/plans/20260701T143211-myp204.yml
## 2026-07-01T14:32:11Z — MYP-204
# ^ ISO-8601 timestamp + ticket ID. Appended by spec-writer at end of run.
- **Status:** completed
# completed | partial | aborted
- **Readiness level:** L2
# L1 = report-only, L2 = assisted, L3 = full autonomy. Set by triage.
- **Archetype phases:** generate-spec → self-healing
# Triage decomposed MYP-204 into two phases: spec first, then code.
- **Duration:** 4m 18s
- **Tokens:** 187,430 total — Haiku: 42,100, Sonnet: 131,200, Opus: 14,130
# Haiku = verifier audit. Sonnet = workers + triage + runtime. Opus = spec-writer only.
- **Cost estimate:** $0.44
- **Artifacts produced:**
  - .planning/phases/MYP-204-spec.md   # 20-section spec
  - src/services/EmailVerificationService.ts
  - src/services/__tests__/EmailVerificationService.test.ts
- **Verifier blocking findings:** 0
# Non-zero here means the verifier flagged issues. Self-healing may have fixed them.
- **Self-healing retries:** 1
# One file needed a corrective dispatch (type error fixed on retry 1 of 3).
- **User overrides:** none
# Records any gate decisions the user manually overrode during the run.
- **Trace:** .loop/traces/20260701T143211-myp204.json
- **Plan archive:** .loop/plans/20260701T143211-myp204.yml

data: The triage agent runs this checklist against every emitted plan.yml before dispatch. A matching anti-pattern either triggers an automatic fix or surfaces a question to the user.
worker-dev writes code. verifier audits it. These must always be separate sub-agents with separate system prompts and model assignments.Flag: any phase where the same agent_id both writes artifacts and runs verification.
data.2-no-attempt-cap#2. No attempt cap: Retry budgets must always be bounded. loop-self-healing enforces max_retries_per_file: 3 by default. Values can be reduced but never set to unlimited.Flag: any phase missing a max_retries_per_* cap.
data.3-vague-triage-output#3. Vague triage ou: Every worker in a plan must have a narrow, explicit scope, a save path, and a rationale. Generic filler phrases are forbidden.Flag: plan contains "handle appropriately", "as needed", "if relevant", or "reasonable defaults".
data.4-l3-before-l1-quality#4. L3 before L1 q: The readiness level must match the project's run history. L3 (unattended) is only appropriate for ticket archetypes the plugin has successfully completed before.Flag: no state.md history for this ticket type, but the plan requests L3. Downshift to L1 and surface the reason.
data.5-shared-state-without-schema#5. Shared: Two phases must never write to the same artifact path without a schema agreement. Each phase owns a distinct output file.Flag: duplicate artifact paths across phases in the same plan.
data.6-mcp-with-write-everything-scope#6. MCP: Each worker's tools list must be the minimum scope needed for that worker's task. Wildcard grants (* or "all tools") are forbidden.Flag: any worker declaring a wildcard tool grant, or requesting new MCP permissions mid-run without surfacing them before planning.
data.7-no-kill-switch#7. No kill switch: Any plan with readiness_level >= L2 or estimated_cost_usd > $1.00 must include an explicit budget cap and approval gate. The orchestrator must also honor SIGINT cleanly for lower-cost plans.Flag: budget cap or gate threshold missing from the plan.
data.8-fixing-flakes-with-code#8. Fixing flak: A test that fails 1 of 3 reruns is a flake, not a bug. loop-self-healing must classify failures before retrying. Persistent inconsistent failures are quarantined and escalated — not patched.Flag: retry policy lacks flake classification logic.
data.9-auto-merge-without-allowlist#9. Auto-m: The plugin produces a branch and a PR description. It never runs git push --force, gh pr merge, or any merge-equivalent command. Tool grants for all sub-agents must exclude merge-on-main capabilities.Flag: any sub-agent tool grant includes gh pr merge, git push origin main, or equivalent.
data.10-no-run-log#10. No run log: Every run must append an entry to .loop/run-log.md. Triage reads it at the start of every run; the spec-writer appends to it at the end.Flag: plan's final phase does not include a loop-state-append step.
data.how-triage-handles-matches#How triage ha: Anti-pattern	Resolution	Auto-fixable (e.g. missing retry cap)	Fix in place and continue	Requires judgment (e.g. L3 without history)	Surface via AskUserQuestion	Still fails after 1 retry	Emit plan with explicit warnings; runtime decides

data: The plugin uses dependency manifests to discover the project's tech stack. Detection walks the repo up to 3 levels deep, skipping node_modules, vendor, .venv, target, build, .gradle, and .dart_tool.
data.node--javascript--typescript#Node / Java: File	Role	package.json	Universal	pnpm-workspace.yaml	pnpm monorepo	turbo.json	Turborepo	nx.json + workspace.json	Nx monorepo	bun.lockb	Bun runtime hint	deno.json / deno.jsonc	Deno
data.python#Python: File	Role	pyproject.toml	Modern projects (poetry, hatch, pdm)	setup.py / setup.cfg	Older setuptools	requirements.txt / requirements-*.txt	pip pinned deps	Pipfile	pipenv	environment.yml	conda
data.go--rust--ruby#Go / Rust / Ruby: File	Ecosystem	go.mod	Go modules	Cargo.toml / rust-toolchain.toml	Rust	Gemfile / *.gemspec / Rakefile	Ruby
data.jvm-java--kotlin--scala--clojure#JVM (Ja: File	Role	pom.xml	Maven	build.gradle / build.gradle.kts	Gradle	build.sbt	Scala SBT	project.clj / deps.edn	Clojure
data.net#.NET: File	Role	*.csproj / *.fsproj / *.sln	Project and solution	Directory.Build.props	Central package management	global.json	SDK version pin
data.mobile-and-cross-platform#Mobile and cro: File	Ecosystem	pubspec.yaml	Flutter / Dart	melos.yaml	Dart monorepo (Melos)	Package.swift	SwiftPM (iOS)	Podfile	CocoaPods (iOS)	app.json / app.config.ts	React Native / Expo
data.infrastructure#Infrastructure: File	Tool	Dockerfile / compose.yaml	Docker	terraform/**/*.tf	Terraform	helm/Chart.yaml	Helm	cdk.json	AWS CDK	Pulumi.yaml	Pulumi	ansible.cfg	Ansible
data.ci#CI: File	Platform	.github/workflows/*.yml	GitHub Actions	.gitlab-ci.yml	GitLab CI	Jenkinsfile	Jenkins	azure-pipelines.yml	Azure Pipelines	.circleci/config.yml	CircleCI
data.classification-logic#Classification logi:
After collecting manifests, the plugin determines project_type and sub_type:
pubspec.yaml present → Flutter
package.json with react-native/expo deps → React Native
package.json with react/next/vue/svelte → Frontend SPA
package.json with nest/express/fastify → Backend Node
go.mod → Go
Cargo.toml → Rust
pubspec.yaml (no mobile dirs) → Dart
pom.xml / build.gradle → JVM
data.no-manifest-found#No manifest found: If no recognized manifest is found, the plugin does not guess the stack. It asks you to declare the stack in a ## Stack section inside CLAUDE.md before proceeding.

data: The plugin does not bundle its own MCP servers. It discovers and adapts to whatever MCP servers you have connected to Claude Code at runtime.
data.1-ticket-source#1. Ticket source:
Fetches the full ticket when you invoke do PROJ-123. Falls back to asking you to paste the text manually if no MCP is available.
Source	Detected tool names	Jira (Atlassian Remote)	mcp__atlassian__getJiraIssue, mcp__atlassian__searchJiraIssuesUsingJql	Linear	mcp__linear__get_issue, mcp__linear__list_issues	GitHub Issues	mcp__github__get_issue, mcp__github__search_issues	GitLab Issues	mcp__gitlab__get_issue
data.2-pr--branch-operations#2. PR / branch o:
Creates the PR after Phase 3 (PR description) is composed. Gated behind pr_creation_gate.
Host	Detected tool names	GitHub	mcp__github__create_pull_request, mcp__github__create_branch	GitLab	mcp__gitlab__create_merge_request	
PRs are always opened as draft. The plugin never merges, squashes, or sets auto-merge.
data.3-notifications#3. Notifications:
Optional. Posts a run summary after completion. Disabled by default via notification_gate.
Channel	Detected tool names	Slack	mcp__slack__send_message, mcp__slack__post_message	Microsoft Teams	mcp__teams__send_message	Discord	mcp__discord__send_message
data.config-schema#Config schema:
# .loop/config.yaml
mcp_integrations:
  ticket_source:
    type: jira           # jira | linear | github_issues | gitlab_issues | none
    cloud_id: ""         # Jira only — auto-discovered if blank
    project_key: "PROJ"
    required: false      # if true: abort run when MCP is unavailable
    update_status_on_complete: true
    status_in_progress: "In Progress"
    status_in_review: "In Review"
    custom_field_acceptance_criteria: "customfield_10001"
    comment_template: "default"
  pr_target:
    type: github         # github | gitlab | none
    owner: "YOUR_ORG"
    repo: "YOUR_REPO"
    base_branch: "develop"
    draft_default: true
    pr_template_path: ".github/pull_request_template.md"
  notification:
    type: slack          # slack | teams | discord | none
    channel: "#dev-loops"
    notify_on: [completed, partial, aborted]
    summary_template: "default"
All token and credential values are read from your MCP server's own configuration — never stored in .loop/config.yaml. Use placeholders like YOUR_JIRA_API_KEY in your MCP server env file.
data.jira--atlassian-remote-mcp#Jira / Atlass:
Ticket fetch flow:
Call getAccessibleAtlassianResources() to resolve the cloud ID.
Call getJiraIssue(cloudId, "PROJ-123") — returns summary, description, custom fields.
Parse summary → title, description → body, customfield_* → acceptance criteria.
Synthesize into a unified ticket_text string.
Post-run status update:After Phase 7 (STATE update), if update_status_on_complete: true:
Call editJiraIssue to set status to status_in_review.
Call addCommentToJiraIssue with run summary, per-agent token table, PR link, and ADR link.
Status update failure is non-fatal — logged as a warning.
data.github-issues--prs#GitHub Issues + PRs:
Ticket fetch:  mcp__github__get_issue(owner, repo, issue_number)
PR creation:   mcp__github__create_pull_request(owner, repo, title, body, head, base, draft=true)
PR list:       mcp__github__list_pull_requests  (for ADR conflict detection)
data.linear#Linear:
Ticket fetch:  mcp__linear__get_issue(id)
Status update: mcp__linear__update_issue(id, fields)
Comment:       mcp__linear__create_comment(issueId, body)
data.runtime-discovery#Runtime discovery: At Phase -1, the plugin runs ToolSearch for each declared integration and caches the discovered tool names. If a required integration is unavailable, the run aborts with a clear message. Optional integrations fall back to manual mode silently.
data.hard-rules#Hard rules:
PRs are always opened as draft. Users mark "ready for review" manually.
gh pr merge, git push origin main, and any merge-equivalent call are never made.
Ticket status is never set to "Done" automatically — "In Review" is the maximum.
Branch cleanup is never performed autonomously.
If no MCP is configured, the plugin falls back to manual ticket text with no behavioral change.

data.overview#Overview: loop-generate-spec transforms a raw feature idea or development ticket into a complete, validated 20-section spec. It uses a Haiku sub-agent to refine the idea, a Sonnet sub-agent to validate the result, and writes the spec under .planning/phases/<slug>/spec.md — the contract that all implementation workers receive.
data.when-to-use#When to use: Invoked automatically by loop-runtime as Phase 1 of any development ticket. The user never calls it directly. It never produces code — its sole output is the spec (and an optional api-contract.md when the ticket involves an API surface).
data.inputs#Inputs: Field	Description	raw_idea	Ticket text verbatim	repo_root	Absolute path to the project root	run_dir	Intermediates directory (/tmp/loop-runs/<ts>/)	adr_decision_summary	(Optional) Locks spec §10 with the upstream ADR decision	adr_acceptance_seeds	(Optional) Seeds spec §8 and §11 from the ADR's acceptance criteria	adr_technical_gaps	(Optional) Populates spec §4 as BLOCKING prerequisites
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Location	Spec file	<repo_root>/.planning/phases/<slug>/spec.md	API contract	<repo_root>/.planning/phases/<slug>/api-contract.md (when applicable)	Phase report	<run_dir>/loop-generate-spec-report.md	
The validator returns one of three verdicts: PASS, PASS_WITH_WARNINGS, or BLOCK. A BLOCK verdict triggers up to 3 re-validation cycles before the skill stops with status: blocked.
data.example#Example:
# Triggered automatically for a development ticket, e.g.:
# "Implement email verification flow for new user sign-up"
#
# Produces: .planning/phases/email-verification-flow/spec.md
#           .planning/phases/email-verification-flow/api-contract.md

data.overview#Overview: loop-orchestrator implements the Orchestrator-Workers pattern: it fans out N independent sub-tasks to parallel worker agents in a single wave, collects their intermediate artifacts, and returns a summary to the runtime. It never synthesizes the final deliverable — that is the spec-writer's job.
data.when-to-use#When to use: Triage selects this archetype when a phase contains N independent sub-tasks — multi-source research, side-by-side comparisons, per-item analysis — where worker K's output does not depend on worker K-1. For sequential pipelines where each step consumes the previous step's output, use loop-plan-execute instead.
data.inputs#Inputs:
A phase spec block from plan.yml:
phase: research
archetype: orchestrator-workers
workers:
  - id: w1
    model: sonnet
    prompt: "Research option A — scope, maturity, trade-offs."
  - id: w2
    model: sonnet
    prompt: "Research option B — scope, maturity, trade-offs."
artifact_dir: /tmp/loop-runs/<ts>/intermediates/research/
acceptance:
  - one .md per worker, 200-500 words
  - each must cite at least 2 sources
budget:
  max_total_tokens: 100000
  per_worker_timeout_s: 300
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Description	Artifact files	One file per worker at artifact_dir/<worker_id>.md	Runtime summary	List of artifact paths + token usage + any soft warnings	
Workers that fail are retried once with the failure mode in the prompt. Persistent failures are surfaced to the runtime — never silently dropped.
data.example#Example:
# Triage selects orchestrator-workers for:
# "Compare Vite, Turbopack, and esbuild as build tools for our frontend"
#
# Dispatches 3 workers in parallel → 3 research artifacts
# spec-writer assembles the final comparison document in Phase 5

data.overview#Overview: loop-plan-execute implements the Plan-Execute pattern: a sequential pipeline where each step consumes the previous step's output. It uses TaskCreate/TaskUpdate as a durable state machine, supports replanning on step failure, and returns all step artifacts to the runtime without synthesizing them.
data.when-to-use#When to use: Triage selects this archetype when steps have explicit dependencies — step N needs step N-1's output before it can run. Typical examples: research → outline → draft → revise, or extract → transform → load → validate. For independent tasks that can run in parallel, use loop-orchestrator instead.
data.inputs#Inputs:
A phase spec block from plan.yml:
phase: pipeline
archetype: plan-execute
steps:
  - id: s1
    name: research
    model: sonnet
    prompt: "..."
    output: /tmp/loop-runs/<ts>/intermediates/research.md
    depends_on: []
  - id: s2
    name: build-matrix
    model: sonnet
    prompt: "Read {{s1.output}}, extract structured comparison matrix."
    output: /tmp/loop-runs/<ts>/intermediates/matrix.csv
    depends_on: [s1]
replan_policy: on_failure
budget:
  max_total_tokens: 80000
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Description	Step artifacts	One file per step at the configured output path	Task list state	completed / failed / skipped per step	Replan log	Present when any step triggered replanning	Token usage	Cumulative across all steps	
When a step fails, a small replanner worker decides: retry with an adjusted prompt, skip the step if downstream is still viable, or escalate to the user. Maximum 2 replans per phase.
data.example#Example:
# Triage selects plan-execute for:
# "Research our top 3 auth libraries and write a recommendation doc"
#
# s1: research.md  →  s2: matrix.csv  →  s3: recommendation.md
# Each step reads the previous step's file before running.

data.overview#Overview: loop-self-healing implements the write → verify → re-dispatch cycle for code deliverables. Each worker writes in an isolated git worktree, the runtime runs the project's own verification commands after every wave, and any failure triggers a targeted corrective dispatch — only for the file the error points to, with the full stderr as context.
data.when-to-use#When to use: Triage selects this archetype when a phase needs to implement files with executable acceptance criteria (§8 of the spec names the verification commands). It is language-agnostic: commands come from the project's own task runner, not from hardcoded assumptions. Not suitable for prose deliverables.
data.inputs#Inputs:
A phase spec block from plan.yml:
phase: implement
archetype: self-healing
spec_path: <repo_root>/.planning/phases/<slug>/spec.md
api_contract_path: <repo_root>/.planning/phases/<slug>/api-contract.md
repo_root: <absolute path>
files:
  - path: src/utils/rate-limiter.ts
    action: NUEVO
    follow_example: src/utils/logger.ts
    worker_id: w1
  - path: src/middleware/rate-limit.ts
    action: NUEVO
    follow_example: src/middleware/auth.ts
    worker_id: w2
verification_commands:
  - cmd: "npm run typecheck"
    label: typecheck
  - cmd: "npm test -- --testPathPattern=rate"
    label: tests
max_retries_per_file: 3
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Location	Implemented files	Branch loop/<ticket_id> (never pushed)	Self-healing report	<run_dir>/self-healing-report.md	
The report includes a verification matrix showing the status of each command per corrective round, per-file retry counts, and worktree cleanup status.
data.example#Example:
# Triage selects self-healing for:
# "Implement rate limiting middleware — 100 req/min per API key"
#
# Wave 1: 2 workers write files in isolated worktrees
# Merge → typecheck fails on rate-limiter.ts
# Corrective dispatch: worker for rate-limiter.ts only, with stderr attached
# Re-merge → re-verify → all green
# Branch loop/TKT-421 created

data.overview#Overview: loop-adr generates a new Architecture Decision Record (ADR) for tickets that involve a meaningful architectural choice. It performs codebase analysis, surfaces trade-offs, detects PR/MR conflicts, and derives acceptance criteria from existing tests — all before a single line of implementation is written.
data.when-to-use#When to use: Invoked automatically by loop-runtime as Phase 0 when triage classifies a ticket as containing an architectural decision: new technology adoption, API design, data model changes, infrastructure choices, or cross-cutting concerns. The user never calls it directly.
data.inputs#Inputs: Field	Description	ticket	Verbatim ticket text	title	Short imperative phrase (inferred if not provided)	remote_adrs_url	Optional URL to another repo's docs/adr/ for style reference	repo_url	Optional repo URL for open PR/MR conflict scanning	output_lang	es or en — inherited from project detection, never asked	run_dir	Intermediates directory (/tmp/loop-runs/<ts>/)
data.outputs#Outputs:
Output	Location	ADR file	<repo_root>/docs/adr/ADR-NNN-<slug>.md	Phase report	<run_dir>/loop-adr-report.md	
The ADR feeds directly into loop-generate-spec: its ## Decision section becomes spec §10 (locked), its acceptance criteria seed spec §8, and its technical gaps populate spec §4 as blocking prerequisites.
data.example#Example:
# Triggered automatically when triage detects an architectural decision, e.g.:
# "Adopt Redis for distributed session caching — replace current in-memory store"
#
# Produces: docs/adr/ADR-007-use-redis-for-session-caching.md
